And you shouldn't let yourself get, you shouldn't pressure yourself into guaranteeing where you don't need to or guaranteeing what you can't. And so much of what we do, so like I have gotten asked about copywriting services more than anything else because they're paying a lot of money and they're actually getting work product, right? So what happens if I give you $125,000 and you write all this stuff and we run the ads and they don't work? What's your guarantee? And the guarantee is there is none, right? First of all, there's too many variables I can't control. So they may run the ads, but did they answer the phone? They may run the ads, but did they send the package out? And sometimes I'm going to fail. And so the only guarantee in that arrangement is, because I'm fee plus royalty, so as long as the clients pay in the royalty and testing, I'll keep tweaking. So basically as long as you're continuing to spend money trying to make it work, I'll keep redoing it trying to make it work. But there ain't no guarantee it's ever going to work. And I can't recall losing one. You know, I just can't. Okay. I have a really quick question. Uh-huh. My question is now that I royally screwed it up and tricked off a couple of clients, how do I go back to now having a position of authority? You know, I mean, I went to them. I did all these things, I think, because I was kind of, I wanted to prove that, yes, I do really do know your stuff. And look, I can do it. So now that, you know, I believe I can do it, how do I get back to that point? Or should I just get rid of those three clients and then move on? I don't think either is necessary. I mean, it's not like you've done it with 50. So how big of a nightmare can they be? There's only three of them? Well, they're a nightmare because it's way less than what I should have charged them. Oh, yeah, well, you know, everybody's got those. Okay. I mean, everybody's got them. I got grandfather clients who I shudder when I hear from them. Well, because they're paying so much less than anybody else is. I don't have many, but I got them. I mean, look, I don't think it's a big deal. I think you, you know, you don't make whatever you now view as mistakes. You don't make them again. You don't go out of your way to stimulate additional business from these people. But you, of course, fulfill all your obligations and you lean on them for non-monetary compensation, testimonials, be on a tell seminar, come present what we've done together at a chapter meeting. You know, you make a big deal out of the fact that they are paying less. Now, if they become a time problem or a management problem, then you got to sit down and have the come to Jesus talk. You know, I mean, look, you got me when I was new and, you know, that's fine and paying X, but everybody else is paying Y. And they're not. They can't get, you know, here's the rules of engagement that they're operating under. Here's the rules of engagement you got to operate under. And if you can't operate under those rules, then we should part company as friends. By the way, nine out of ten times when you have to have that conversation, nine out of ten times they repent, apologize, and get back into good behavior. So nine out of ten times when it becomes the equivalent of show me your exercise, you didn't do your exercise, you can't be here as a patient. They start doing the exercises. So nine out of ten times when you have to sit somebody down and say, you know, hey, look, you know, you can't be sending me 12 facts as a day, each one with a separate question on it. You know, you can't, this is not the way we play. And if this is the only thing that will make you happy, then we need to find you somebody who will work with you on that basis, but it can't be me. Nine out of ten times they whip back into shape.
